A really unique side scrolling metroidvania souls-like.  The first thing I'll say is that I really liked this game. That being said, it's far from my favorite, and I didn't always like it. The game started off fun, became annoying almost boring, but then it began to grow on me and I liked it by the end.  The leveling souls like nature of the game is pretty straightforward. You don't lose currency on deaths, so it does take away some of the fear factor. There is a robust class system with talent trees and abilities that allows you to create a unique experience and would allow for some replayability. I found a lot of the options useless, but perhaps they were just not my play style.  the map is pretty big and is annoying to travel around. Fast travel isn't unlocked until pretty much the end of the game, so it made going back for things annoying. There was no reason not to introduce this earlier in the game.  The combat can be challenging at times, but you can get pretty good at it. You can get pretty powerful, but the bosses always felt meaningful to me. They weren't so hard that I couldn't beat most of them on the first attempt, but some took a few tries. There are harder versions of each boss that you can take on again, which I thought was interesting. They have new mechanics and are certainly more challenging, but I decided not to invest the time to beating them all.  There are a lot of ways the story can go depending on choices you make. You can backup your files if you want to get all the endings in one go around, just make sure to turn off steam cloud backups before you get to the point of no return, or else the game won't recognize your backups.  The story is a little confusing at times, but I think that was intentional. There are some elements that seem tacked on, and perhaps cliched at times, but overall, it's an interesting story. I wish there had been a bit more information, as the game left me with some questions. I assumed I could watch a lore video or read a write up to get a full account, but I could not find one.  The game has a lot of content and the asking price is more than fair. If you are on the fence, like metroidvanias and souls-like games, and the game is on sale, I would just go for it.

The story feels unappealing and bland. While the game attempts to mix humor with its otherwise dark atmosphere, the humor often feels out of place rather than providing a welcome contrast. For example, the Death character—typically portrayed as arrogant and imposing—suddenly takes on a comedic role working in a kitchen out of boredom. This moment felt jarring and undermined the game’s overall tone.  Gameplay-wise, the developers couldn’t decide between parry or timed-block mechanics, so they included both. Each offers a distinct mechanic, but the two don’t synergize, and your build can’t effectively benefit from both simultaneously. This lack of focus makes the combat system feel inconsistent. The game would have been better served by choosing one mechanic and refining it.  Leveling is also disappointing. The game features four talent trees: one general, two tied to classes, and a fourth I didn’t reach. Unfortunately, the talents across the trees are uninspiring. In most games with talent trees, I find myself excited to plan my build and pursue specific upgrades. Here, the talents feel average at best, leaving me indifferent about progression. Instead of motivating me to strategize, the system feels like an afterthought.

I initially bounced off hard from this game, although I can't tell why. Now, several years later, I decided to give it another try after watching a few episodes of a let's play on YT, and I found myself having fun.  Pros: - Great pixel art - Really well done voice acting, and great casting  - Story is really interesting and what starts as dark fantasy, eventually becomes even darker and eldritch. I must say that the twists and turns surprised me a few times. What initially may be unclear, will eventually be explained and revealed, so you don't need to piece the story together from item descriptions like in other Souls-likes. - Combat is actually fun once you learn enemy patterns and more importantly - your own attacks and their timings. - Boss fights offer some challenge, but once you learn their patterns fighting them turns into a well-choreographed dance. I never had to make more than five attempts per boss except in the case of some of the main late-game fights and the heroic versions of boss fights (those are brutal)  Mid: - Exploration. As usual in metroidvanias, once you unlock new traversal abilities, more and more of the map will open to you. However, here, the game could have used a better map marker system that would allow the player to enter some description. All we get are a marker that we can place anywhere but no way to describe what's there. Also, exploring rarely leads to interesting finds. - Obtuse puzzles. There are few puzzles in the game, and a couple of them are so convoluted that I have no idea how anybody could do them without a guide.  Cons: - Heroic boss fights. These are just complete and utter bull. Not fun at all, unlike the normal bosses you fight throughout the game. - Parallax Nightmare Room. I get the idea behind it and it fits the premise and the circumstances, but it is just NOT fun. - Journey's End. Awful, awful zone. - Magister's Labirynth. I loathe platforming gauntlets with spikes, so this area was a nightmare.

[i]Originally posted on [url=https://www.dragon-quill.net/quick-reviews-7/]Dragon Quill.[/url] Comments are enabled there.[/i]  This is 2D Dark Souls. The core mechanics are almost identical: Point-build level up system, the same currency is used for both levels and items, there’s stamina and dodge rolling and estus flasks. It makes a few simplifications I appreciated: There is a corpse run mechanic, but you only lose an estus flask and not all your money, and you can skip it by recalling your lost flasks for some currency. There is less equipment and stats are simpler to understand — in particular, armor simply boosts your stats directly instead of providing needlessly-convoluted defensive stats. Additionally, if you lose to a boss, you get a portion of its money drop based on how close you got to beating it, which is a nice way to throw a bone to struggling players. However, it does not simplify nearly enough to fix all the problems with Dark Souls. There are still way too many mechanics to keep track of during pitched battles, and I frequently forgot to use my abilities and items. The upgrade system is really awkward when there are multiple weapons of each type, one of which is objectively worse than the others; there really should have just been one of each as in Unworthy. And as in Dark Souls, fast travel is withheld until far too late in the game.  Many parts of the game also felt half-baked or like they were pulling in different directions. The classes are wildly unbalanced because strength is used for both shields and weapons while the other offensive stats are only used for weapons, and there are way fewer finesse weapons than strength weapons on top of that. The final segment of the game features a segment where you’re supposedly trapped in an area, but you can still teleport out to faff about with sidequests whenever you want, which kills the pacing. The text is also riddled with grammatical errors that have no business being in a commercial game.  Story is “What if Dark Souls, but more grimdark,” complete with the nonsense death cultism but justified with even stupider cliches. “Immortality is bad because it attracts an evil god” is a non-sequitor. What does that have to do with real life? What are you trying to say with this? Why was this worth telling a story about? I may have hated the death cultism in Dark Souls but at least it was engaging with real religious philosophy. This is just nothing.

Death's Gambit: Afterlife stands out with some of the best pixel art in gaming. The environments are intricately detailed, each with its own unique aesthetic, creating distinct atmospheres that immerse you in the world. The game's music further enhances the experience, complemented by top-notch voice acting that gives emotional weight to characters, even if their time on screen is brief. There’s a quirky charm here—moments of unexpected humor juxtaposed with the game’s heavy themes of death and acceptance, creating a unique tone that may not resonate with everyone but sets it apart from typical dark fantasy games. The narrative also offers multiple endings, appealing to those who find the story engaging, though some might find it frustrating that a bad ending can be triggered if you die too often. Unfortunately, cutscenes are unskippable, which hurts replayability.  Combat, while simple, is satisfying and varied. Each weapon type has its own unique combo, making them feel distinct. They also come with special abilities that consume Soul Energy—a resource gained through combat that ties into class-specific talents. Players can wield two weapons at a time, and there are numerous weapon variations that tweak damage types or offer unique perks. Impressively, the game maintains balance between weapon classes, something many action RPGs struggle with. This is due to the effective scaling of damage with player stats and the usefulness of at least one ability for each weapon type.  Boss battles are the game's highlight, presenting a fair challenge that rewards skill and patience. However, their difficulty might be off-putting to players who get easily frustrated. For those seeking an even steeper challenge, the Heroic Mode allows you to refight bosses with added mechanics. I personally enjoyed this mode, but some Heroic bosses lean toward being frustrating rather than fun—throwing in additional enemies or spamming projectiles. While Heroic Mode is optional (though defeating five Heroic bosses is required for the best ending), it's still a divisive feature. On the downside, the boss battles heavily encourage investing in attack stats to deal with their high health pools efficiently, but even with that flaw, they remain the most compelling aspect of the game.  The game succeeds as a Metroidvania by guiding players effectively to new areas, avoiding the aimless wandering common in the genre. Backtracking to old areas with newly acquired abilities to find hidden items is rewarding, thanks to fluid movement mechanics that feel good during both combat and exploration.  For completionists, the game hides secrets so well that stumbling upon them naturally is unlikely. Discovering the secret boss Xyarlohatp, for example, would be nearly impossible without a guide (and good luck pronouncing that name!). Despite this, finishing the main game without external help is manageable.  The game does have its flaws. Attribute progression is disappointingly linear—most players will focus on attack stats to maximize damage—and equipment upgrades are material-heavy, making farming tedious. Yet, despite these issues, Death’s Gambit: Afterlife remains one of my most replayed games. Its charming presentation, challenging combat, and well-designed world draw me back, even if I wish for more streamlined skips for cutscenes and simpler access to secrets. If you’re up for some punishing yet rewarding action, it's worth a play.

8/10 story, 10/10 gameplay.  The game has a pretty top notch story that is held back by odd choices to certain mechanics and some changes to the game in the Afterlife update didn't really make it any better or worse.   The gameplay is a really solid balance of platformer and souls-like. There are such a large number of ways to customize how you play, that my only real complaint becomes that replaying the game without new game plus feels empty in comparison. granted, it only takes 30 minutes to get to almost anywhere in the game, if you know what you are doing which makes the game feel super satisfying.   The game has some metroidvania elements, but they are kept very minor.  Here is a spoiler for the story related to my complaints about the afterlife edition and how it ruins some of the messages of the game in my opinion. [spoiler] So the main crux of the game's story revolves around what immortality means. If someone(the main character) is immortal, can they be trusted to end *other* immortality? Is someone weaker as a person for having the ability to die and become revived with the same frequency as their enemies? Before the update, the main way to see this effect was to become mortal. So Death, at the start of the game will give the main character (Sorin) a contract. Until the immortality of the kingdom is destroyed, Sorin will come back to life over and over again if he was to lose his life. Pre-update, A lot of characters would call Sorin weak in character and in morals for choosing to stay as an immortal. Post-update, those same characters show indifference to him, regardless of whether Sorin has or has not been revealed to be immortal. Pre-update the implications of being immortal was that every death was slowly spoiling Sorin's soul and in the end, even if he didn't die once as an immortal, he would be a husk that would be too dangerous to be left alive. Post-update, there is no mention of Sorin's soul. none.[/spoiler]  Rant over. Now the important part:  Buy this game if you want a solid metroidvania with a solid story. I've played through the game, scouring secrets and playtesting tons of different builds. Theorycrafting the lore by testing tons of different things with the gameplay. Finding a... considerable amount of bugs. Most of the bugs were fixed Post-Afterlife update but some still remain(such as the "breaking contract in certain areas will cause NPCs to not react to your new mortality and will not change how they act and will softlock the values in their story, so they might be hostile even though they shouldn't be).

After grinding a bit and trying the content after the base game final boss.... it is garbage. There is no differentiation between base game postgame, Afterlife endgame and Vados update, which makes everything a mess. Either you were here day 0 or you don't know wtf is going on. There is no logic for what path to follow, the wiki has the bare minimum and most posts about the game were pre-afterlife update. To top it off, exploration in this game is extremely tedious, with the in-game map being almost useless and and a huge lack of tp or just fast traversal options, so just cant simply backtrack the entire game without wanting to blow your head off.  It is also a pure grindfest. Most bosses don't feel like you can get good at them, you just have to farm more lvls or upgrade gear. Also, any other class that isn't soldier is apparently trash, considering that the best way to play this game is by perfect blocking.  Once again, I recommend to complete the base game right up until Endless, and then just forget about this game. That part felt like real progress and the bosses weren't as much ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Just thinking about doing the 100% of this game fills me with pure dread.

Excellent Souls-Like Metroidvania game (with a few glaring flaws) requiring many deaths and mastery of dodge/parry/block mechanics to complete.  This game grows on you. I spent the first hour or so completely afraid of everything, saving and restoring my health every few minutes and learning the controls as well as each enemy’s attack patterns. It took me a while to understand the characters in the game, though the story is straightforward: you die, and death makes you immortal (explains the respawning) in exchange for your help destroying the source of immorality (a little ironic, isn’t it?). It's a very difficult game, but with RPG stat growth and ability increases, I was able to get good enough to defeat the bosses. I will admit, however, that I used the Alt-F4 exploit (i.e., close the game before your death animation ends to avoid a Will increase) because I wanted to see the best ending and didn’t want to get soft-locked out of it. After many hours and beating enough heroic bosses for the good ending, I beat Thalamus and felt a huge sense of accomplishment.  The Positives -This game’s controls become second-nature pretty quickly despite the many moves you have. I tended to stick to dodging over parrying or blocking and primarily used the traps, combo attack, and primary ability. Because of the number of options, though, I would have to relearn the button mappings for whatever weapons or abilities I had, needing to stand in one place and practice hitting the buttons to reacquire new muscle memory. But once you get it down, you become very effective at dodging and hitting enemies and bosses.  -The visuals are fun—I love modern high-definition pixel art games that combine classic visuals with modern visuals. The enemies and bosses are all interesting-looking, and the game always has something happening on the screen—from nice backgrounds to attacks and environmental affects.  -The designers did a fantastic job of making this game an easy-to-learn, hard-to-master situation combined with a you-will-go-from-sucking-to-winning-flawless-victories gameplay against bosses. I have to admit that I get frustrated by getting killed and being terrible the first time I fight every boss, but the idea that in the first 5 minutes you think the boss is impossible and then an hour later you can win without getting hit is outstanding.  -The various play styles made possible by the different classes is quite an achievement. I picked the Acolyte of Death, which gave me a few more save points in front of boss areas and a weapon that hit more things around me in a semi-circle but sacrificed a small amount of DPS compared to other classes. I enjoyed the various approaches that you could take to the game and used a couple of different strategies for my character across the game. I also liked how I could trade in plumes for a higher damage rate, which allowed me to try to kill the bosses more quickly (my preference) vs. trying to outlast them.  -Because of the combination of RPG, Metroidvania, and Souls-Like combat, the game feels very rewarding. It’s not just “scroll right and kill things” but instead always thinking about the next item, the next talent point, the next ability, the next piece of equipment, the next level up, and the next area or boss. There is always something to do or find until the end—and, of course, it loops if you want it to.  The Negatives -This game is incredibly difficult—Souls-Like games are intentionally designed to make you learn the enemy patterns and do 1-3 dodges, parries, or blocks per attack you get to make on them. Therefore, it’s not for the mindless hack-and-slash kind of player. Instead, you get a quick look at the enemy’s pattern before you die, you go back again and master that, and then you learn the next part of the pattern. On the positive side, it does train you to treat failure as a learning opportunity and become Zen about it—a good life skill to develop.  -I wish that I didn’t have to take the precaution of Alt-F4ing out of the game on death so that I wasn’t worried about my Will score being too high to get the good ending. I would’ve preferred to play the game as intended, but if I had played through it straight, beat all of the bosses on Heroic, and still had too much Will to see it, I would’ve resented the game forever. So I did what I had to do for my peace of mind. It’s possible that I would’ve qualified regardless (since I ended the game with 3 Will), but I didn’t want to take the chance. It would’ve been better if Ending A was solely based on having enough Heroic boss wins vs. a deaths-or-purple-damage-per-Heroic-boss score. And purple damage causing Will increases is a terrible game mechanic.  -The weapons, armor, and abilities for your class are few and far between. I came to be fine with that for the weapons and armor because you could upgrade them with stones, but it did kind of make it feel like a game of “get more damage and health to beat the next boss”. Nothing else really seemed to matter to me—I never really noticed my stamina boosts and barely noticed my ability cooldowns. I really only used two abilities that I got within the first hour of gameplay for the whole game. So, in that regard, it was just get new armor with one more FIN/VIT point -> upgrade it with soul stones (only using higher-level stones on end-game equipment) -> fight next boss.  -The Talent Points were probably the most useless that I’ve ever seen in a game that had talent trees. At one point in the game, I had stacked 9-10 talent point because using them was not going to help me beat the next boss. I never chose a second class because none of the abilities would help me. The only useful ones increased my damage or soul point increases to use my primary ability.  -When you put the above together, it really exposes the game when you can boil it down to “get more damage and health to increase your survivability and decrease your time to kill the bosses until you can win” as your strategy. So the RPG elements in some respects simply added needless grinding to reduce the number of hits required to beat bosses.  -I played the game after the update, and like many games that have base versions and then enhanced versions, it felt like so much was thrown at me. I think players who played the original, loved it, and then played the update would like it the most, but as a new player with the DLC there was so much going on with nothing being explained that I just defaulted to searching for the next thing to do.  -The difficulty fluctuations were blatant and frequent. I’d go to an area where it was near-impossible, then go to an area where it was easy. I’d fight a boss that was near-impossible and needed to level, and then I’d go elsewhere and it’d be easy (likely because I was supposed to go there first).   Overall, the game is fantastic, but it does have many flaws that might hinder your enjoyment of it multiple times throughout. I ended on such a high because I went from getting slaughtered by the boss over and over to winning relatively easily over the course of a few hours, but I still recall the issues I had throughout. I was willing to overlook them all because of how much fun the game ended up being, but it’s hard not to notice how cryptic everything is (e.g., everyone in my town was killed and I had no idea why), the difficulty fluctuations, the lack of any useful abilities or talents, and the fact that you really just needed to invest all of your points in your attack score (Finesse for an Acolyte). That being said, committing to the play style and beating Heroic bosses ended up feeling rewarding—while I only needed 5 to see Ending A, I beat more than that for fun. Note that Ending A has many endings like Lord of the Rings: Return of the King, so I was ready to be done when it was over. Great game that I’d recommend, but you need patience for its Souls-Like gameplay and understanding of its flaws to enjoy it.

It feels as though it was made by gathering a bunch of ideas and throwing them all in a blender with no regard for how they would work together. It's obvious which games the devs drew from with no regard for the context of those games' other mechanics. The plot in the early game and to a lesser extent the art style just feels like a Blasphemous rip off without the religious angle that gave Blasphemous some level of unique intrigue. As someone who didn't enjoy Blasphemous - if you were thinking about getting this game and don't own Blasphemous get that instead. If you have Blasphemous and enjoyed it maybe get this when it's on sale but don't expect the polish and cohesion it did.   Combat against non-boss enemies is nothing special. Then against bosses,  it can get daunting and tedious when you are so slow and animations are anything but fluid while the boss is so fast with a long reach and usually at least one minion.   Honestly I could forgive all of that and leave at least a more neutral positive review but:   1. The animations for boss deaths feel intentionally slow as if to increase the chance of you taking one last hit and dying when you think you've won.   2. There is no fast travel until the very end of the story. This  makes a vast majority of the game involve a tedious, boring slog to navigate the map.   3. When you die you drop one of your phoenix plumes (think dropping an estus flask in dark souls for comparison). The only ways to get it back are go find it again or pay essence. Retrieval can be tedious as there is no proper fast travel and the map is frequently generic enough to lose track of where thing are. Paying essence costs the same amount of essence that a level up would cost. This essentially punishes more for dying after you have leveled up a bit because the cost of recovering plumes is the same cost as a level up (and of course level ups cost more as you do so). So retrieving plumes becomes more tedious as time goes on as you are forced to go get it or grind up essence to retrieve the plumes. This issue is all the more clear when you reach multiple bosses that require you to initiate the fight to get your plume back.   4. With no fast travel the need to back track for items is that much more tedious and pads content- nothing more. The backtracking doesn't feel the same as metroidvania backtracking usually does. Metroidvania backtracking generally involves being able to solve a puzzle after getting a new power. This just feels like "oh yeah, I can jump up there now".   5. Even if you could excuse everything else- the WILL mechanic is the single most asinine, stupid mechanic ever put into a game. It literally arbitrarily blocks you from the best endings that you have gone through so much to get.   Elaboration involves spoilers: The will mechanic increases every time you die, every time you are hit by purple spells, every time you are hit by some green spells, every time a certain enemy type dies and respawns in a later zone.   To receive the best endings you MUST fight 5 of the same bosses you have already beat but buffed way up (Heroic bosses) and your WILL stat must be below 100. It starts at 100 at the very beginning of the game. Each time you beat a heroic boss for the FIRST TIME AND ONLY THE FIRST TIME- your will stat goes down by at least 20. That doesn't change that you have a limited number of deaths before you just don't get the good endings.   6. Late game bosses are what I can only describe as visual clutter. Late game bosses generally feel like the genre abruptly changed to bullet hell.   7. The plot begins really basic and underwhelming and then the setting suddenly pulls garbage out of left field. Literally everyone is in medieval gear - then suddenly there's a boss with  a modern fire arm. Totally collapsed what little immersion there was. Then the plot is generally all over the place and feels like it's trying to be more "deep" than it really is.   If the reason you are here is because it's a metroidvania- don't. There are so many better options. If you're here for a souls like- you would be better off replaying dark souls on a no hit, no shield run if you want to have challenge less artificial. And once again, there are already so many better options.   In summation: This is literally "What if I made dark souls but made it so that every time you die, you lose an estus flask and there's a score that can arbitrarily tell you that you don't get the good endings, and you had to fight the bosses again but harder to do anything about it for no reason. Then let's make the fast travel pointless because the game is practically over by the time you get it (much later than in dark souls 1 that many complained about)."   1/10 The only reason it's not zero is the lumberkins were neat I guess.

[h1] The TL;DR Positives: [/h1] [list]     [*]Gorgeous visuals.     [*]Classes/abilities are fun to experiment with and most of the collectibles are at least worth finding to try out.     [*]Excellent dialogue and voice acting (for the most part.)     [*]Great sound design.     [*]Story is somewhat predictable, but is still thought provoking and a joy to experience, even for subsequent playthroughs.  [/list]  [h1] Now for Criticism. But before going any further, here are My System Specs: [/h1] Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-7800X CPU @ 3.50GHz   3.50 GHz 16.0 GB (15.7 GB usable) GeForce GTX 1080 Ti Not high end modern system to be sure, but decent enough that you would think that I shouldn't have any technical issues running this game.  [h1] Performance & Stuttering Issues: [/h1] [list]     [*]You can do some online searches for how to address these issues, but the TL;DR is that Ti cards have been known to have particularly bad performance issues with Death's Gambit. But they can be addressed... to a degree.  Regardless here's what you should know going into this game.      [*]FPS tracking will not help you because the performance issues typically don't directly affect the frame rate at all. During some of the worst instances of performance/stuttering that I experienced, my frame rate was consistently at a steady 55FPS at worst and capped out at 60FPS during most of my play time.      [*]Death's Gambit Afterlife is a memory hog and you can expect it demand at least 50% of your system memory during combat especially boss fights, which are particularly poorly optimized.     [*]Steam Link is likely to make these issues even worse, and if you haven't setup your steam link settings to maximize game performance already, you should, because this game deserves to be played on a big screen.     [*]The temptation to button mash can be overwhelming, and even with button buffering it can be weirdly  effective because input delay/failure bugs can occur during combat. However, button mashing can make performance and stuttering issues worse, especially with player or enemy  abilities/attacks that generate VFX with more complex animations. [/list]  [h1] Input Delay/Failure Bugs and Button Buffering: [/h1] [list]     [*]You are guaranteed to experience seemingly random button input failure and or delay at least once and it typically occurs during intense boss fights. I usually encountered the issue during the heroic forms of bosses. Possibly one of the best examples of this problem is the Tundra Lord Kaern boss (even during the 1st fight,) because the player needs to alternate between ground slams and air dashes. It's a boss design that would otherwise be interesting if it weren't so plagued by this issue. The Phoenix Rider boss is another great example of how performance issues combined with button input delay/failure can really burn the player experience.      [*]Like many modern games after the early 2000s, Death's Gambit Afterlife has mandatory button buffering imposed on its controls. This is to discourage button mashing and to keep the player from bugging out the game with button mashing or sequence breaking button precision karate like they're playing in a GDQ. Button buffering, in my opinion, is dumb and a cheap cop-out from designing more stable gameplay. It is already bad enough that air-dash and ground-slam are locked to the same button press, but the button buffering combined with button input delay/failure bugs can cause massive frustration even when game performance is running perfectly.      [*]Unfortunately, there is no fix that I know off to effectively address these button input delay/failure issues. You can try remapping your controls to see if that helps you. Best suggestions I can offer is do what you can to correct any performance issues first as these will only make your input delay/failure experiences even more soul-crushing, and then be prepared to die around 5 times at the minimum to any bosses where these issues manifest with the goal of getting a feel for the optimal button input strategy and timing rhythm for said fights.  [/list]  [h1] Final Thoughts: [/h1] Death's Gambit After Life is absolutely worth playing for any souls-like fan and for any 2-D Action Platformer fan. If you like this game, try Salt & Sanctuary 1 too; you'll find similar appeal and issues with both.  Personally, I would not bother trying to "git gud" at this game, because so much of the late game content challenges are poorly optimized and some bosses are so cheesy they give some of the worst ATLUS games boss fights a run for their money. Instead, if you want to unlock different ending options that are challenging, such as the breaking the death contract and no-save-point-run, save yourself some time/energy and install a mod to make the game easier. I certainly won't judge you for it.

[h2]The "Will" mechanic: how to take a perfectly fun game and ruin it[/h2]   This game features a stat called Will, which is a measurement of how much control Death (the character) has over you. It goes up every time you die, and the only way to lower it is to fight "heroic" bosses, which are stupidly, unreasonably difficult rematches with bosses you've already beaten. I like the idea of these rematches, but I [i]hate[/i] the way they're implemented here. If your Will is too high when you get to the endgame (which it definitely will be on your first playthrough), you have to go back and do these "heroic" fights or else you don't get the best ending.  This totally ruined the game for me. I was actually having a lot of fun right up until I got to the "point of no return" just before the final boss. It was only then that the game told me I wasn't "strong enough" and should go back and do some of these "heroic" fights or else it wouldn't go well for me. So I tried a few, and...well, let's just say there was no more fun to be had.  It's not just the insane difficulty that kills it for me - I'm pretty sure I could beat these fights if I wanted to devote several more hours to them - it's the [I]tedium[/I] of it. It's the sense of "I already beat this guy!!! Why do I have to do it again?!?" I strongly feel like these "NG+ on steroids level tough" types of battles should only ever be optional post-game content. By locking preferable endgame material behind them, all you do is take away the sense of accomplishment the player got from beating them the first time. Not to mention that it's just a cheap way to pad out the length of your game, and it disrupts the pacing of the game by stopping all forward progress.  I still haven't attempted to beat the final boss yet, as I'm still several "heroic" fights away from being "strong enough" in the game's opinion. At this point, I'm so frustrated with those fights that I might just stop. If I can't get a good ending in a reasonable way, I'd rather have NO ending.  This might be the fastest I've ever changed opinions on a game, and I'm bummed. I really thought I'd found a gem.

And before you go "just rebind your keys", the default controls of a game should be as equally playable as any other configuration. It should not be my job as a player to fix your shoddy development.  Oh and also this game is a kind of generic metroidvania that while having some pretty cool bosses, locations and moments (Thalamus stands out) doesn't do a lot to differentiate it from the pack in the way that say, Blasphemous does. Even the ZeroLenny reference can't save this one.

精品游戏，值得花时间体验，画风唯美，配乐优秀，配合游戏剧情塑造出了无与伦比的宿命感和史诗感，有多种武器选择，可以在一局游戏中通过洗点的方式体验几乎全部流派，地图很大，隐藏要素很多，几乎不可能100%进度，因为传送给的很晚，所以有些地方需要重复跑图，拖延了游戏节奏和时间，boss颇有难度并且有一些很有设计感，打完普通boss之后，可以重新挑战进阶版boss，绝大部分可以说一周目之下，很难打得过，但是要想有好一点的结局必须挑战5个进阶boss，需要看看视频和攻略才好打一些，最后一个boss设计的感觉真的很震撼，总体来说，值得推荐。

When Dark Souls 1 and Undertale have a baby , we have Death Gambit: Afterlife.  This has to be the biggest surprised for me this year as far as Metrodvania goes, because this game starts up so weak / confusing but ends in such a strong note. The beginning of the game seems to copy paste Dark Souls 1 a bit too much, to the point that people might put off this game because it's almost 1 to 1 recreation of DS1. The initial combat is a bit too easy/ shallow too with weak hitting physic.  HOWEVER, after around mid-way point of the story, the game completely turns into its own thing with such an intriguing story/ characters / location/ boss design/ and map design. The map design and boss design becomes so creative and masterfully crafted in the later part of the game, totally become its own thing. In the end game Afterlife portion, the game completely blown me away with all the 4th wall breaking and fighting dimensional God. It's almost as if I'm playing Undertale true ending all over again.  The story/ character/progression system/ bosses/ map design completely blow Salt and Sanctuary / Ori/ Bloodstain/ Metrod Dread out of the water if you look at the complete package of this game, making the best Souls-like Metrodvania out there in the market.   If you are a fan of Dark Soul 1 and Metrodvania genre, this is a 10/10 must play game. You only have to endure like the first 5 hr of slog to get to the real meat of this game.  Please give it a try, you won't regret it.

Overall, ok, some good core combat though definintely with some problems. Buy for a quick playthrough, especially if on sale. Don't stick around for the replayability though.  The levels/worlds tend to feel small, enclosed, and short. Overall exploring the world is pretty meh... Not much wonder there, or sense of exploration sadly, a bit interesting for a few key story areas, but often you can't go back and even then nothing really breathtaking or out there. So overall ok world design, but just fell a bit short of really being something special sadly.  The story is... good, I'd say, probably the main reason to play it. I'll avoid spoilders, but it's probably the high point of the game. Though... if feeling stuck, bored, frusterated, etc... just keep going with the main story, don't do the sidestuff, if you can't reasonably handle it yet. The story probably wont be over, and if it is, save it for NG+. Heroic challenges are, pretty poorly ballanced and don't match up with the rest of the game well honestly.  Bosses, most are pretty good, if frankly too easy and simple on normal difficulty. Then there's heroic though... these range from being just pretty hard, requiring a ton of grinding to have the stats to reasonably face them, to absolutely obscene and questionable in design and ballance for being in the game on the first playthrough. I'd say it's just not worth it, even if you like punishing enemies and good challenge, a lot of them are, at least on the first playthrough, just not good. And even on later ones some bad design issues stand out.  Bugs, like with Cusith boss, heroic. The part that the first time he drops his lighting ball each fight, when they hit the game freezes momentarily. When it un hitches, it will now sometimes change things... like ressetting my upwards velocity from a jump, and removing my ability to double jump that time... so two thirds of the time that fight starts I will, when he first uses that move, auto take a good third plus of my health in damage just because of the game. Or the first time I beat it... how it bugged out and didn't die with zero health, ket hitting it, a bit franticly, wouldn't die... This was the moment I just gave up on trying to do heriocs. I did bother on him another few times just to finish him and confirm the bug, didn't happen next time I got him down.  And I'd argue most of my deaths there are caused by not being able to see clearly the tiny exploding parts or timer bars on the lightning bombs, since they zoom way out for that fight, more than they need to, to the point you can't clearly focus on all the tiny yet most important details. Design like this is just really annoying and infurating frankly... Bugs and bad design all in one there... And between the part that they are a jump from difficulty of 2, to like 9 to 10, being really damn sharp in a game, the part they designs keep making me just frusterated like with that camera work, or some RNG on enemy skills that really stretches what's possible to deal with without taking a hit, the bugs that crop up just were the nail in the coffin. In the end, I don't recomend herioc fights in this game, and without them... it's honestly pretty short. Though the added length of them is kind of false since that's just you dying over and over or grinding upgrades/leveles elsewhere to face them.  Now to be clear, most fights are much better than that one, many do not have any reliable bugs that regularly frusterate me. And it is overall mostly good, but there are just those few issues, some repeatable like that one, others less frequent like dropped inputs (like a boss room I enter, and about a quarter of the time I get a dropped input within ten seconds, an attack, a jump, something will drop. Got in the habit of when I got in just randomly jumping and attacking before starting the fight to see when it would trigger and getting it out of the way before the boss fight, very annoying and made an already long grindy herioc fight take even longer.  Equipment... very unbalanced seeming. Tons of halberds and such... but if you want axes and went for that build, screw you. No new weapons for you... well, till a bit after halw way through the game, you get one... it's not much better, but a bit. There's a notably better one from beating the game in X method that does self harm you when using it, but suffice to say, you will be using the starter axe for 70% of your game run. Don't even bother expecting to find any others... And good luck dumping all your valuable annoying to farm resources for upgrades into the starting axe but still just feeling underpowered once you start heriocs with it...  Also a lack of skills for the axe... honestly it just made my experience really fursterating, mixed with sub par world design and exploration and I wasn't feeling a lot of enjoyment out of exploring and progressing in general. Getting covered in alternate weapons/items for other builds, but nothing for mine... really started to feel like the devs just had it out for me for having picked the wrong starter class...  So ya, it's worth a quick playthrough if it's on sale I'd say. But replaying or doing the higher diff stuff... I'd not recomnend. Shame, it really does have a LOT of potential and good points in here, just the bad ones keep me from really falling in love with it.

Und wieder ein Metroidvania. Und wieder eines, das ich nicht auf dem Schirm hatte und das trotz frappierender Ähnlichkeit zu anderen Genre-Vertretern wie Blasphemous einen eigenen Charakter und einen ebensolchen Charme hat.  Dieses Mal wird die Geschichte um die stetige Wiederbelebung unseres Helden so erklärt, dass er einen Pakt mit dem Tod geschlossen hat und dieser ihn eben wieder zurückholt, sollte er im Verlauf des Spiels das Leben verlieren.  Abgesehen davon darf man aber rein inhaltlich gewohnte Kost erwarten. Eine eher kryptische Geschichte führt uns durch ein Land voller angriffslustiger Kreaturen bis zum letzten schicksalhaften Kampf und tritt dabei wie so oft in dern Hintergrund, denn das Spiel funktioniert völlig unabhängig davon.  Die Spielwelt ist ein weit verzweigtes Netz, das aus unterschiedlichen Arealen besteht, die nahtlos ineinander übergehen und Verbindungswege in andere oder bereits bekannte Gebiete bieten.  Aufgrund der Nicht-Linearität ist es auch hier der Regelfall, dass man innerhalb eines Gebiets oft nicht weiterkommt, weil man entweder die passenden Gegenstände nicht besitzt (Schlüssel, Siegelsteine und dergleichen) oder die notwendigen Fähigkeiten nicht erworben hat.   So gilt es in den zugänglichen Arealen alles abzusuchen und vor allem die jeweiligen Endgegner zu besiegen, die dem Spieler meist den nötigen Fortschritt bringen.  Mit Doppelsprüngen erreicht man luftige Höhen, die man in einer zweiten Ausbaustufe noch deutlich erweitern kann, der Vorwärtsschub lässt größere Distanzen in der Luft überbrücken und der Bodenstampfer sorgt für den entsprechenden Durchbruch an diversen Stellen.  Überall stellen sich Feinde in den Weg, allesamt mit unterschiedlichen Angriffsmustern, Stärken und Schwächen, die es herauszuarbeiten gilt. Konfrontationen sind dabei am besten auf ein Duell auszurichten, wenn möglich, denn bei mehreren Widersachern zugleich kann man schnell in die Bredouille geraten.   Die Endgegner hingegen sind eine Klasse für sich. Ob übergroß und mächtig oder super-agil und mit magischem Brimborium um sich werfend: jeder von ihnen erfordert eine gewisse Lernphase, um als Spieler gut auszuteilen und möglichst wenig einzustecken.  In mehreren Phasen wechseln die Bosse entweder kurzzeitig die Taktik und zwingen den Spieler in die Defensive oder sie erweitern ihr Angriffsrepertoire um zusätzliche Attacken, die eine Anpassung der Spielweise erfordert.  So gibt es beispielsweise eine Scharfschützin, die sich in einer Übergangsphase in den Hintergrund verzieht und den Spieler aus unerreichbarer Ferne aufs Korn nimmt, während dieser die Deckungsmöglichkeiten in der Hintergrundgrafik nutzen muss, um nicht zu viel Schaden zu erleiden.  Ein andere Boss wechselt auf einer wackeligen Plattform ständig die Seite, wodurch sich der Untergrund entsprechend neigt und den Spieler abzuwerfen droht, wenn er nicht gegensteuert, was es schwieriger macht, ein gutes Angriffsfenster zu finden.  Und wenn ein durchgeknallter Magier uns in einer eigenen Dimension zum Zweikampf auffordert, fühlt man sich schon fast wie in Terraria, wo man meist eine ähnliche Arena aufbaut, in der man schnell die verschiedenen Ebenen wechseln kann.   Bis zu diesem Punkt ähnelt das Spiel deutlich den Konkurrenten und reiht sich qualitativ wunderbar dort ein. Wer Last Faith und die beiden Blasphemous Teile mag, wir auch hier fündig. Die Optik ist vergleichbar und obwohl es auch hier wieder die "Pixelgrafik" ist, hat man sich nach wenigen Minuten daran gewöhnt.  Fast schon Standard ist ein guter Soundtrack und das ist auch hier gegeben, wobei ich diesen im Vergleich zu den genannten Konkurrenten dann doch eher hinten einordnen muss. Das ist aber immer noch extrem gut. Der Sound hätte für meinen Geschmack noch etwas mehr Druck vertragen, denn gerade zu den teils übergroßen Waffen gehört auch die akustische Wucht. Das fehlte mir etwas, doch auch das ist nur eine Frage der Gewohnheit und fehlende Wucht ist noch weit entfernt von schlecht.   Etwas anders als gewohnt, funktioniert das Progressionssystem. Durch das Erlegen von Feinden erlangen wir Punkte, die man an Ruheorten, die hier Grabsteine sind, gegen Levelaufstiege eintauschen kann. Mit jedem Aufstieg erhöht sich der Preis für den nächsten Level und erfordert somit entweder das Besiegen von stärkeren Feinden oder entsprechend vielen Gegnern der leichten Kategorie.  Da mit der Ruhephase alle normalen Gegner wiederbelebt werden, ist auch hier das Grinden möglich, indem man eine lukrative Passage immer wieder spielt und sich so ein Vermögen anhäuft.   Der Stufenanstieg hilft später aber immer weniger, denn man bekommt eben immer nur einen Punkt auf eines der Attribute gutgeschrieben, während die Kosten stetig steigen. Daher gilt es vor allem, die richtige Waffenklasse für sich zu finden und darin die beste Waffe für den eigenen Spielstil.  Diese sollte dann regelmäßig verbessert werden, um den Schaden zu erhöhen. Und hier geht das Spiel deutlich in die Richtung der Souls-Spiele, denn Waffenklassen sind auf jeweils ein Attribut spezialisiert. Meine Klasse war beispielsweise die der Großschwerter und das Attribut dafür ist Kraft.  So habe ich einerseits verschiedene Großschwerter mit mir herumgetragen und ausprobiert, bis ich meinen Favoriten gefunden hatte und nebenbei den Kraft-Wert immer weiter erhöht und damit den Schaden, den ich im Kampf anrichte.  Andere Waffen, wie Dolche, erfordern eher Technik und mit Intelligenz lässt es sich gut auf Magie setzen. Einen Allrounder aufzubauen wäre sich auch möglich, ist aber wie so oft nicht sinnvoll, denn von allem etwas zu können bringt weniger Erfolg, als der Fokus auf eine Sache. Passive Talente darf man durch Besiegen von Bossen in mehreren Bäumen freischalten und zum Spielstil passende Stärkungen bestimmter Aktionen erwerben.  Fähigkeiten hingegen kann man entweder finden oder käuflich erwerben und in drei Schnellzugriffsplätzen verteilen, sodass man die drei als nützliche empfundenen per Tastendruck einsetzen kann.  Ein Beispiel ist hier der Feuerschlag, der aber erst funktioniert, wenn man genügend Fähigkeitenpunkte durch normales Kämpfen angesammelt hat  Und dann wären da noch die Verbrauchsgegenstände. Geheilt wird mit Federn, die man im weiteren Verlauf in diversen Variationen findet. So hat meine Feder, der ich bis zum Ende die Treue gehalten habe, mit der Heilung eine grüne Aura erzeugt, in die ich mich hineinstelle und zeitbegrenzt weitere Heilung erfahren kann.  Die Feder ist es auch, die man beim Ableben verliert und am Ort des Todes wieder aufsammeln kann.  Schafft man es nicht, an die Feder heranzukommen, kann man sie am Ruhepunkt auch gegen Punkte zurückkaufen, sodass nichts dauerhaft verloren geht.   Und so hat man alle Zutaten beisammen, die ein gutes Metroidvania ausmachen. Von der Länge scheint es etwas kürzter zu sein, wie die anderen genannten Titel, doch es hängt auch maßgeblich davon ab, wie entdeckungsfreudig man ist.  Über ein paar kleine Schnitzer sehe ich gerne hinweg, wie etwa dass man sich erst sehr spät im Spiel zwischen den Grabsteinen teleportieren kann. Die Reisen zu Fuß sind zwar meist schnell überbrückt und können in der Reiterpassage mit einen Pferd beschleunigt werden, doch der direkte Weg ist eben viel bequemer.   Man könnte noch mehr schreiben, doch ich belasse es bei einer klaren Empfehlung an alle Genrefans und Kennern der konkurrierenden Spiele. Ich hatte extrem viel Spaß, war stetig motiviert und habe selbst Bosse bezwungen, die mit mir beim ersten Zusammentreffen direkt den Boden gewischt hatten. Man lernt. Man lernt schnell. Und man freut sich über jeden Sieg und jeden kleinen Fortschritt, bis man beinahe unbemerkt im großen Finale steht.

[h1][b]Review[/b][/h1]  [b][i]Death's Gambit: Afterlife[/i][/b] is a very polished MetroidVania with punishing but solid combat, good exploration, a very nice RPG aspect, and a sense of uncovering that is quite rare to encounter. It has some minor snags, though.  [h3][b]The Highlights[/b][/h3]  [list] [*]You have lots of classes to choose from when you start. And after you level up, you can distribute points in your build, like in a classic RPG.  [*]The RPG aspect is heavy; you have lots of equipment and items that you can use and get passive buffs. Also, there's a skill tree that you can branch into different skills and abilities.  [*]The pixel art is not overly detailed and is somewhat rustic, but I like it. It's difficult for me not to like pixel art.  [*]I didn't like the combat at first (as I don't like the "[b][i]Souls[/i][/b] combat" where stamina is needed to act), but after a while I adapted to it, and it became pretty fluid.  [*]The dialogue has voice acting; it's nice; normally you don't find voice acting alongside dialogues (at least I don't), so it's cool when I come across it. [i]But, weirdly enough, the protagonist has no voice lines. I got the true ending, but I still don't get this design choice.[/i]  [*]You have a lot of freedom; the game allows you to engage in ways that feel like they're organic. For example, you can attack pretty much any NPC—[b]kill Bast![/b] Also, the exploration was very satisfying; I was always finding new things and items for wherever I was exploring.  [*]The game is pretty challenging, the enemies are unforgiven, and the damage taken is substantial. And the bosses are equally hard. [i]Although there are some difficulty spikes, they are quite preposterous[/i]. [/list]  [h3][b]The Drawbacks[/b][/h3]  [list] [*]One major negative point is that there is no fast travel until you reach the endgame. Like, c'mon, who thought this was a good idea?  [*]There are several little minor nuisances in details that I noticed, like: some voice lines are missing from the dialogue. And other little technical things that I observed that were not polished.  [*]But I gotta say... there are some design choices that are just perplexing. Like, I'm all on board for obscure secret puzzling stuff, but that invisible platforming puzzle to unlock the secret boss is abolutely atrocious. [b]One thing is trying to make something difficult; the other is creating an obtuse, stupid section that sucks all the fun away from the game.[/b] [/list]  [h3][b]Conclusion:[/b][/h3]  [b][i]Death's Gambit: Afterlife[/i][/b] is a pretty big MetroidVania (40+ hours), with lots of content, challenging battles, prime exploration, huge RPG elements, and... it's FUN (for the most part); it's not perfect in totality, but it is a very solid game. The score can't be anything less than:  [h2][b]8/10[/b][/h2]

Short: -Game is so and so -Small bosses fun, most others cheesy -No teleport travel until towards the end -Story goes from "ok" to towards the end, vague and disjointed -Needs polish -Skills, classes, leveling is sorely underwhelming -Good voice acting -Lots of hidden paths that are vital for progression  The game is so and so. Get ready to get lost a lot and have to look for a walkthrough or wiki which will be very limited. The smaller humanoid bosses are actually fun to fight. Most of the other bosses, very very cheesy.  Every time you die, your "WILL" increases, if you reach 100 or above in "WILL", you are automatically locked out of the "True Ending" and honestly it's the desired ending. The game doesn't tell you or even really hint at what the consequences are for dying and increasing "WILL". This WILL can be lowered ONLY by fighting harder versions of the bosses you encountered, "Heroic" Challenges. Once you beat all of the "Heroic" bosses, you no longer have any way whatsoever to lower the "WILL". The decrease counts only the first time per "Heroic" boss. The WILL doesn't come into play until near the end of the game.  What isn't well known, or even documented at all, is that the deaths of mobs ALSO build towards the "WILL" count. This claim comes from me verifying the increase every time I kill 2-3 mobs. Mobs that spawn as part of boss fights also count towards "WILL" increase.  There is no manual save, nor is there any save files you can manually delete in your computer to revert to any previous points if you mess up.  If you get this game, don't do as I did: 1st run, got halfway, got lost for an hour, quit for a year, came back, beat all the bosses and "Heroic" bosses, got to the end, realize I got locked in because dialogue told me, (literally the game chooses this moment in time to finally tell you), finish game and go NG+. Messed up by not realizing "Heroic" only counts once, can't revert save, locked myself in again to the undesired ending. No method of decreasing "WILL", so now given up on the game. I beat it so I guess it can be buried.  The grind was fine first go around. You lose your abilities every NG+, so you have to grind again to get through the run, tedious. Grind with no way to remedy progression errors, with hours put in... Not worth NG+.  I should've realized after getting stuck for an hour because of the map and cryptic layout that I should've refunded.

[h1]Review zu Death's Gambit: Afterlife[/h1]  [b]Statt Bonfire, Death's Shrine.[/b]  Selten habe ich ein Spiel gespielt, das so [i]mediocre[/i] ist... Ich will auch gar nicht erst wissen, wie viel schlechter die Ursprungsversion des Spiels zur Veröffentlichung gewesen sein muss (also die "Nicht-Afterlife-Version"), schließlich hatte diese nochmal deutlich schlechtere Reviews eingefahren, als die Neuveröffentlichung namens Afterlife. Ganz ehrlich? Mich macht dieses Spiel wütend, weil es so viel mehr Potenzial hätte... Es ist das bereits alte bekannte Spiel, dass ich schon [url=https://steamcommunity.com/id/vazeyo/recommended/369070/]in meiner Slain: Back from Hell Review[/url] erwähnt hatte:  [quote=Auszug aus meiner Slain: Back from Hell Review]"Künstlerische Aspekte, wie Musik, oder Ästhetik sehen fantastisch aus, aber das Spiel fühlt sich nicht gut an."[/quote]  Und genauso hier. Verschiedene Departments des Entwicklerstudios haben hier verschieden gute Qualitätsarbeit geleistet. Die Musik ist IMMENS gut. Deutlich besser als ich dem Spiel zugetraut hätte. Das hätte so auch aus einer High-Budget-Serie stammen können, die ein ähnliches Setting hat. Der Pixelart sieht auch recht schick aus, auch wenn ich diesen für meinen persönlichen Geschmack etwas zu fein finde. Mir haben auch die Charaktermodelle bei Dialogen gefallen, auch wenn nicht jeder Charakter konsequent ein Porträt erhalten hat... Die Synchronsprecher sind wirklich talentiert und man merkt, dass man alles aus ihnen rausgekriegt hat! Aber... das war es dann schon... Nachfolgend ein paar weitere Gedanken und Gründe, warum ich mich zu einem vorzeitigen Abbruch des Spiels entschieden habe, bequem als Auflistung:  [list]     [*]Cutscenes sind nicht pausierbar     [*]Ich finde es merkwürdig, dass der Protagonist stumm ist, während alle weiteren Charaktere vertont sind. Möglicherweise gibt es dafür einen Grund innerhalb der Welt, aber so weit hab ich nicht gespielt, um das herauszufinden.     [*]Die Navigation auf der Weltkarte ist viel zu hektisch und kann nicht justiert werden, sodass zu platzierende Markierungen nie wirklich da sind, wo ich sie gerne hätte...     [*]Irgendwie wirkt das Spiel unfertig? Während des Spielens gab es viele Features, die ich mir gewünscht hätte, aber nicht vorhanden waren. Es gab auch viele Kleinigkeiten, die einfach merkwürdige Designentscheidungen waren. Insgesamt zu viele, um sie alle aufzuzählen. Während des Spielens sollte es dem Spieler allerdings auffallen, was ich konkret meine.     [*]Kämpfe sind immens langwierig und langweilig, weil viele Gegner viel Leben haben. Levelerhöhungen bzgl. des eigenen Schadens helfen da nur bedingt weiter. Es fühlt sich zudem auch nicht komfortabel an zu kämpfen und ist von der Steuerung sogar noch klobiger und lästiger als [url=https://steamcommunity.com/id/vazeyo/recommended/774361?snr=1_5_9__402]damals in Blasphemous[/url]. Ich kann hierbei nur an einen Meister seines Fachs verweisen: Dead Cells. Ein flüssigeres 2D-Pixelart-Soulslike-Kampfsystem wird man auf dem Gamingmarkt bisher nicht finden können und das ist halt einfach die Messlatte, sofern man das Spiel kennt. Death's Gambit kann da im Vergleich nicht mithalten, was sehr schade ist.     [*]Obwohl ich den Pixelart angenehm finde, sind es die Pixelart[u]animationen[/u] hingegen nicht... und ich muss dabei ganz ehrlich gestehen: ich finde es merkwürdig, dass ich das kritisiere, weil ich das bisher in keinem anderen Videospiel kritisiert habe. Death's Gambit ist dahingehend wirklich ein Phänomen für mich... Besagte Animationen sind allerdings nicht wirklich gut, verbesserungswürdig und wirken roboterhaft. Wie gesagt: ich kenne da besseres á la Dead Cells... [/list]  Warum also ist das Spiel so mittelmäßig bis schlecht? Ich kann dabei nur eine Vermutung abgeben: Death's Gambit ist das erste und bisher einzige Spiel von "White Rabbit", dem Entwicklerstudio hinter Death's Gambit, dementsprechend ihr Debüt-Titel und das merkt man halt leider auch... im negativen Sinne. Man merkt, dass sie nicht so viele Erfahrungen hatten. Das sie vermutlich nicht so viel Budget erhalten haben, oder, dass ihnen Möglichkeiten fehlten. Wer weiß. Und für Ihren Debüt-Titel muss ich sagen, dass das Spiel wirklich gut ist. [b]Zumindest für einen Debüt-Titel![/b] Aber abseits davon, und ohne diesen Hintergedanken, kann ich dieses Spiel nicht guten Gewissens empfehlen. Man wird dieses Spiel irgendwann schlichtweg vorzeitig abbrechen. Das zeigen schon ganz gut die Achievements: Nicht einmal 40% der Spielerschaft hat den zweiten Boss besiegt und diesen findet man innerhalb der ersten 2 Spielstunden. Das sollte schon augenöffnend sein. Dann besuche doch meine [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/34784076/]Steamkuratorseite![/url] ;-)[/code]

Death gambit - лучшая метроидвания в которую я играл. А я играл в Hollow Knight, 2 части ори, Salt and Sanctuary, Salt and Sacrifice(правда быстро дропнул как и ори), dead cells и тд.  В любой игре есть свои минусы и плюсы, поехали разбираться. Плюсы: Хороший дизайн, ну за это просто респект, игра очень красивая и локации сделаны очень классно. Получаешь эстетическое удовольствие при игре.  Исследование мира, я лично ни разу не задушился при исследовании мира как в том же Hollow knight. Да, я не могу не сказать, что враги бывают однотипные, но по своему строению локи очень отличаются и это очень классно. Ты че то открываешь, какие-то плюшки интересные. Это не просто проход до босса как в Hollow knight или солях, здесь ты именно постепенно открываешь путь. Условно там кнопочка, тут область в которую нужно встать и тд. Всё сделано по красоте, единственное даже добавив карту, я очень мучался и не понимал как пройти в некоторые места :(.  Анимации, анимации сделаны очень четко. Прям конфетка, как персонаж бьет, как враги бьют или боссы. Это не просто дубовые анимации, здесь враг именно пытается тебя ударить, в отличии от каких-нибудь дарк соулсов(он захейтил дарк соулс, да начнется срач).  Интересные механики, которые очень нравятся и которые работают между друг другом: Хилки, теперь при смерти вы не теряете души, а теряете хилки и вам придется возвращаться и поднимать. Падает при смерти всего 1 штучка. Но при этом, вы можете зайти на Идол Смерти(Аля костёр) и убрать вообще хилки. Какой от этого толк? Вам баффают урон, за каждое перо которые вы убрали получаете +10% к урону. В итоге когда у вас будет 12 перьев например, у вас будет +120% к урону это дохера. Сундук золотой и сундук крови. Золотой сундук, находится на всех локах и иногда после боссов, можно открыть если вы не потратили не одну хилку. Обычно там лежит какой-то лут, оружка или камень для прокачки(аля титанит). Сундук крови, это сундук который будет выхиливаться постепенно, и не смотря на этот хилл вы должны бесконечно его бить. Такой мини босс можно сказать, и вы получаете очень даже неплохие ресы за него, лут такой же как и в золотом принципе.  Кувырки, УРА ХОТЬ КТО-ТО ДОДУМАЛСЯ, не делать фреймы в кувырке, а просто дать фрейм неуязвимости на всю анимацию кувырка, спасибо.  Минусы:  Сюжет, я не знал куда его отнести, поэтому наверно отнесу скорее в минус. Он спокойный, линейный и скорее рассказывается история именно главного героя нежели мира. Историю мира можно узнать, посмотрев вступление при запуске игры, а если хотите узнать из чего он состоит и кто им управлять и так далее, тут можно углубиться. В сюжете есть повороты неожиданные, но я их скорее ожидал и не получил удивления. В принципе, стабильный сюжет.  Прокачка персонажа..ну, очень странно. Есть много показателей, стамина, хп, ловкость сила и тд это понятно. Ловкость повышает урон оружиям которые от ловкости, и сила тоже. Но есть такие навыки как...быстрота? Уменьшает кд способностям персонажа. Но почему она баффает урон? В игре очень удобно сделана таблица в которой при прокачке показано что прокачивается и быстрота..качает АБСОЛЮТНО ВСЁ НА 2%...та же ловкость качает только ловкость на 6% где-то. Выносливость качает тоже всё на 2%. Получается, если хочешь быть докачем, либо не прокачивайся или качай только хп. Короче очень странная система, думаю им надо было сделать конкретику, а не подобное.  Героические боссы. Я не имею никаких претензий к ним..кроме хп. Да Героические боссы это усиленная версия боссов, вы можете победить их и получить хороший лут, вещи и тд и это нужно для некоторых концовок игры. У них добавляются новые атаки, фазы меняются и всё классно. Но у них слишком много хп, я проходил игру с косой и убивал много героических боссов(можно посмотреть по ачивкам в стиме) и хп вагоны я боссов по 10-15 минут убивал, хотя был на максимальной прокачке которая позволяла игра на данном нг. На нг+ такой проблемы нету, ты очень сильный и даже иногда перекаченный и очень легко дерешься с ними, легче чем на обычном нг.  Перемещение по карте, иногда я по карте смотрел куда мне идти и вообще не понимал как туда дойти. Поэтому мне было чаще всего удобно, самому искать туда проход даже не смотря на карту. Ну это вкусовщина скорее.  Музыка, меня не зацепило. Не могу припомнить от кого я мог кайфовать, наверно только Амульваро и всё. Но на самом деле это вкусощина, я вообще не люблю музыку в играх, единственные игры откуда я помню музыка: Hades 2 и Celeste)   Вот такой обзорчик вышел, конечно в нём не все моменты..обзор вышел бы слишком длинным. Я новичок в обзорах и если что напишите, какие моменты лучше тоже в отзывах упоминать, я буду редактировать. Спасибо что читали :3.

Очень достойная и сильно недооцененная, на мой взгляд, метроидвания. На релизе была очень кривой и недоделанной, но сейчас является пожалуй одним из лучших представителей жанра. Не Hollow Knight конечно, но точно не хуже условной Ender Lilies (которая на порядок известнее). Да, у игры до сих пор есть огрехи. Очень странные и зачастую почти бесполезные таланты дерева прокачки, предложения в описаниях налезающие друг на друга, не самое удобное меню инвентаря. В общем та самая "кривизна". Да и коротковата по длительности для требуемой цены. Однако ощущается игра приятно, как в частности (боевка), так и прогресии в целом. Боссы хоть в целом и несложные, но красиво сделанные. Да и есть "героический" режим битв, в котором они могут и сдачи дать вполне серьезно. Ну а самым приятным аспектом для меня стала повествуемая история. Начинавшаяся как более-менее стандартное приключение в фентези мире со временем она начинает уходить в Лавкрафтовщину, местами отдавать Андертейлом, удивлять необычными поворотами и "ломать 4 стену". И всё это сопровождается отличной музыкой и прекрасной озвучкой (от малоизвестной инди в этом плане ждешь гораздо меньшего). В общем любителям жанра брать в коллекцию однозначно.
Одни из самых худших боссов в истории соулс-лайк и метроидвании, кривое управление, скучная прокачка.

Como um fã de souls like, metroidvania e de jogos com estilo pixel art, digo que se você se identifica assim, esse é um ABSOLUTO must-play.  É tanta arma e habilidade diferente, tanto secret, vários endings, a jogabilidade é fluida e responsiva, particularmente acho a dificuldade bem justa apesar do jogo ser bem punitivo com alguns debuffs. Tenho tanto pra falar bem nesse jogo mas principalmente sobre como ele é LINDO. Os cenários, a coreografia dos personagens, as artes, TUDO nesse jogo é uma obra de arte em pixel art.  Já zerei esse jogo umas três vezes em outras plataformas, a última foi quando saiu a DLC e eu decidi comprar por aqui. Sinceramente já estou com vontade de jogar de novo. A história é interessante (e melhorou muito com o Afterlife), as músicas são muito boas, e o jogo é divertido ao ponto de ser viciante. Não só recomendo como acho que merece muito mais reconhecimento, os desenvolvedores colocaram amor puro nessa obra.

This was a fantastic metroidvania that borrows HEAVILY from dark souls. the classes seem to be very different although i could be wrong since i only played one. I played the wizard and while i enjoyed that very much i felt like I was at a disadvantage compared to other classes (more specifically for boss fights) due to how the magic works. to cast a spell you must combine different elements by "casting them" to create different spells based on your combo of elements (identical to Outward, invoker in Dota, Magicka) but "casting" each element takes time, this meant that i never really had to time to create any spells during most boss fights since you just dont have time so i wondered if the other classes would have a much easier time taking on bosses. I was however still able to beat all the bosses and their harder versions so i guess its not a big deal. I very much like pixel art style so that was a big plus for me. Death is a great character and is hilarious. Having a lot more rpg elements in a metroidvania was refreshing and compelling especially since i seem to be on a metroidvania marathon lately  really really liked this game and enjoyed it even more than hollow knight. I only had a few gripes with the game: 1. It would have been nice to find more than 2 weapons for the wizard, although they do function different 2. Unfortunately the game borrows the extremely boring and lazy level up system from dark souls that is good in exactly 0 games 3. (this is maybe a bug) When you use the monuments that summon your horse it obviously spawns near the monument however interaction with the monument takes priority over interacting with your horse so you cant mount, you can sometimes avoid the problem by standing at the edge of the monument when you summon it but i still found it a sort of blatant thing to overlook. 3 1/2. The horse also serves almost no use except for moving through a single hallway a bit faster  i found these to be minor issues and this game was really a 10/10 for me especially since i got it in the humble bundle

Death's Gambit: Afterlife est une version améliorée du jeu d'action-RPG Death's Gambit, développé par White Rabbit et publié par Serenity Forge. Cette édition enrichie apporte des améliorations significatives au gameplay, au contenu et à la qualité de vie, offrant une expérience encore plus captivante pour les joueurs.  Le jeu se distingue par son esthétique visuelle magnifique et ses animations fluides, qui plongent les joueurs dans un univers sombre et intrigant. Le style artistique, combiné à une bande-son atmosphérique, crée une ambiance immersive qui renforce le ton épique et mélancolique de l'histoire.  Le gameplay de Death's Gambit: Afterlife est exigeant mais gratifiant, mêlant des éléments de combat précis et de stratégie. Les combats de boss sont particulièrement mémorables, nécessitant une maîtrise des mécaniques et une planification minutieuse. Les nouvelles classes, compétences et armes ajoutent une profondeur supplémentaire, permettant aux joueurs de personnaliser leur style de jeu et d'explorer différentes approches tactiques.  Les améliorations apportées par Afterlife, telles que les nouveaux niveaux, les ennemis, et les ajustements de difficulté, enrichissent l'expérience globale et corrigent les lacunes de la version originale. La navigation dans le monde est plus fluide et les défis sont mieux équilibrés, rendant le jeu accessible tout en maintenant un bon niveau de défi.  En résumé, Death's Gambit: Afterlife est une version améliorée qui mérite l'attention des amateurs de jeux d'action-RPG. Avec ses graphismes superbes, son gameplay engageant et ses nombreuses améliorations, il offre une expérience enrichissante et mémorable pour tous les fans du genre.

This is a solid Metroidvania game. It seems to hit all the right beats and while it does have some very difficult sections none of it felt super unfair or unachievable.   The story is decent enough too, nothing special, but not too bad either. I have to agree with other reviews that the tone of the story is a little odd, but I think a game like this needed some comic relief as opposed to just being another straight clone of other souls like games similar to it. This game let's you really find your own playstyle and make it work for you as well. The gameplay was just straight up fun. I'd definitely recommend playing this game.

although a forgiving soulslike, it still feels fullfilling to beat bosses and stuff, has a nice story, only downside is that sometimes it just tries too much to be hard, by just giving bosses moves that will drop half your HP, but the combat is nice, takes pratice and it allows for some sick dps'ing

The good: -The movement feels really good. The way you attack , block and dodge feels natural. -You can actually perform perfect blocks, even on bosses. -Lots of different items to choose from . -Several classes to choose from, so a lot of replayability. -You have an endurance stat. There's a gauge bar and every time you block, dodge or attack a part of the gauge gets deduced. If it becomes empty, you have to wait a couple of secods so it starts recharging again. I like this because you have to balance the amount of times you attack with the amount of  times you dodge. -Good artwork, with many different elements. Detailed environments and interesting animations. -The game is hard, but it's not unfair. -Music is really good. -Pretty big map. -Lots of bosses The bad: -The menu looks a bit dated in the graphical part. -There's a stat called will. Each time you die, that stat will get 1 point added. It turns out that if you have more than a 100, you can't get the true ending. And sure, if you beat the bosses in heroic mode you get that stat reduced(you have to beat 5 in heroic mode to get the true  ending anyway) but you could lock yourself out of the true ending if you die too many times. -Certain events are missable. If you leave a character alive at a certain point, he'll kill other characters that you might need for a later quest.  -The story is actually pretty weak, I didn't care a lot about most of the characters.
Not a fan of the voice acting, attacks don't feel well telegraphed. Got it in a humble bundle, glad I didn't get it by itself.

10/10 Absolutely incredible. One of the greatest metroidvanias, just an absolute blast from start to finish. I only have a few issues. First, there's boss runs. Some of them are absolutely heinous. Another is the fact that after finishing the game, you are forced into new game+. This is mainly an issue because you can only start the dlc for the game if you get a certain ending. And, despite me knowing this, I still ended up getting the wrong ending and now I have to replay the entire game again, and I basically need to do it on a new save because ng+ is too hard. Overall, great game, but it's unacceptable that you can just miss out on the dlc that you paid for if you happen to get the wrong ending.  Edit, since this review I have played this game through multiple more times, gotten the true ending, and experienced the DLC. The DLC was not worth the money, it barely adds any content and none of it was very fun. The base game is absolutely worth the money, but the DLC is not.
