Este es un juego que me encanta, incluso con sus inconveniencias y molestias, es una experiencia fantástica.  El juego en sí lo jugué en su primera version, antes del "Afterlife", y sí tenia sus problemas pero me había gustado bastante, una experiencia que nunca olvidé. Ahora se me dio por volver a probarlo con todas las actualizaciones que tuvo estos años. La verdad que a mejorado mucho. La mejor forma de describirlo, es que es un juego con muchas, MUCHÍSIMAS inspiraciones, uno que sea de jugar Souls-like o Metroidvanias puede darse cuenta fácilmente. Por suerte, creo que lo manejaron muy bien, no solo llenándolo de referencias sino también dándole un toque propio distinguiéndolo del resto.   La historia y la música son increíbles, inolvidables en mi caso, y que tuvo sus cambios que los volvieron aún más impactantes. El gameplay lo considero variado y sólido (hasta cierto punto), buena selección de armas y hechizos gracias a las clases que se pueden escoger, muchas habilidades, diferentes árboles de talento y opciones de movilidad, muchas son típicas del genero pero alguna que otra me parecen muy creativas. Visualmente tampoco se queda atrás, las animaciones son muy fluidas, los retratos de los personajes son maravillosos, y los escenarios muy vistosos y diferentes.   Ahora, a qué me refiero con inconveniencias y molestias? bueno... a pesar de la cantidad de actualizaciones, sigue teniendo algún que otro problema con los hitbox y las colisiones, siendo difícil distinguir el rango de los ataques, y aunque el plataformeo si es muy responsivo, las escaleras no tanto. Tambien un problema persistente es el audio, que varias veces se corta o modifica el volumen con bosses y enemigos. En general, es complicado describir el porque el combate no se siente del todo... bien, podría deberse a las animaciones que muchas veces no se pueden interrumpir, o que uno puede superponerse en los bosses o que, de nuevo, los ataques no tengan un rango/duración fácilmente discernibles.  A pesar de eso, me parece una aventura genial, y una a la que le tengo cariño. Se nota todo el trabajo que le pusieron los devs y la perseverancia como para añadir contenido años después de la salida. Puede que no conecte con todo el mundo, pero igualmente, muy recomendado para los interesados en el género Metroidvania.

An excellent metroidvania game that is both at once familiar and fresh to fans of the genre.  Combat and movement are tight, with the usual fare of gaining movement options as you explore and progress into the game (double, triple jumps, air dash, etc.). Thankfully there is no fall damage, and the times you get hit by environmental spikes, traps and DoTs are few and far between, allowing you to progress at a steady pace and not get hung up on sections of the map. Areas requiring very high precision platforming skills are either purely optional, or simply require a specific movement skill (e.g. triple jump or extended dash) to make life easier. This is great because you can quickly move on to the highlight of the game, the amazing boss fights.  Combat is a bit slower paced than other titles in this genre, adhering more to a "Souls-like" slow and tactical timed hits rather than frantic combo spams. Even when using the fastest weapons in the game, daggers and magic tomes, you need to be mindful of the timings of enemy attacks, necessitating moving in and out of range in between bursts of damage. Combat abilities are similarly a bit slower paced, with cooldowns between uses, though this can be shortened with the right stats, gear and setups. The best comparison I can think of in terms of combat pacing is Reckoning: Kingdoms of Amalur (complete with the mysteriously materializing shield whenever the shield is brought out, as well as the perfect block mechanic). It's also great that you can respec your character at no cost, meaning you can experiment with different builds when you come across a cool new weapon or ability, or get so hung up on a boss that you need to rethink your build altogether.  Music is easily one of the best I've heard in a game in a while, and is masterfully used to heighten the emotions of a scene or a fight, be it melancholy, dread or heart-pounding action. Similarly, dialogue is also masterfully done, with great voice acting, lines that are quick and to the point while also conveying the emotions and weight of the plot. Like everything else in the game, dialogue is tight and precise.  Of course, there are some parts that are a little less than perfect, though your mileage may vary on this one. These are not outright negatives to me, but are certainly worth mentioning.  Combat - Boss combat is essentially "Perfect block: the game". Although there are a myriad of strategies and builds that the game encourages you to pursue, when it comes to boss fights (especially the "Heroic" level boss fights, which is when you voluntarily return to fight a more difficult version of the bosses again), overwhelmingly the optimal strategy is to perform perfect blocks and timed counters. This can be problematic for players like me who are much more used to positional and evasive style of boss fights, such as in Megaman, Ender Lilies and Hollow Knight. Fortunately, the base NG game is balanced enough that you can scrape through to the true ending (A) with a bit of effort (except for Amulvaro, that boss was broken and forced me to respec into a perfect block build).  Navigation - The game has an interesting kind of "fast travel" in the form of the Rider's passage, where you can mount a horse and blitz through the map end to end, though you have to unlock sections on foot the first time round. This is great in that it forces you to explore more to unlock all the metroidvania style shortcuts, though it did wear a bit on me towards the end. You do get a teleport from save point to save point ability at a very late point in the game, which can't be any earlier due to lore and plot reasons, but it felt a bit too late in terms of gameplay for me.  Controls - A minor issue, specific to my experience. As my WASD keys are a bit worn out I rebinded the movement controls to new keys, but subsequently found out that some keys in the game are hard locked to specific keys. Noticeable ones are dive kick (so I couldn't reasonably use that skill, though it's no major loss) and dialogue choice inputs (hard bound to A and D, if I recall).  Abilities - The game's multi-class system often has skills that allow you to convert weapon requirements completely, such as being able to equip any bow, and bows now scale with strength instead of Finesse. While very cool on first impression, the weapon specific abilities continue to have requirements based on the original weapon type (so, Finesses requirement for Bow abilities). This effectively means you cannot expect to use those abilities if you stat-converted your weapon skills. Not a major problem if you were simply using the weapon as a sidearm to your main one, but still a bit of a bummer.  Plot - The story in Death's Gambit: Afterlife is very good, though it has components that can come across as self-sabotaging to some players. For one, it has a nice dose of humor, which I appreciate very much given the gravity of the main themes of death, grief and legacy. The issue is when the comic relief parts come too close to a very sombre scene, such as visiting Frupi's restaurant right after fighting and talking with Origa, or simply having gone through a weighty cutscene like the Ione and Dark Knight cutscene, then shortly after accidentally stumbling into Ser Thomas the mighty frog knight.  Another thing about the plot is that it is so very, very meta. While it's not obvious at the start of the game, over time the 4th wall will start to get more and more broken, until a point where some characters will outright talk directly to you as the player. Again, a "your mileage may vary" moment, because while I do feel that the instances the 4th wall was broken was pretty cool and unnerving, it is indeed slightly immersion breaking to the story, and fundamentally changes the way you perceive the plot and the lore of the game world that has been built up to that point.  With all that said, Death's Gambit is still a stellar game which I thoroughly enjoyed playing. It is the closest 2D "Souls-like" game to the spirit of Dark Souls that I have played, in terms of build variety, combat pacing, mood, and the memorable and tough-as-nails boss fights. At the same time, the game respects your time by doing away with unnecessary environmental deaths, and providing many interesting areas to explore. Highly recommended!

查了下定价109，那得是个差评了。 这个游戏整体的逻辑不如死细胞自洽。战斗上希望通过魂游的动作帧数体系和加点体系形成魂like的体验，这使得游戏初期的体验非常好。但是后期很多英雄boss动作抽风和满屏幕乱堆判定的设计让战斗体验非常崩塌。完美盾防和盾反分别对应死细胞的格挡和魂游的盾反，收益也各不相同。但是能比较契合这个机制的boss都在前期，后期只有无尽这个boss是契合盾反机制的。后期本来应该是玩家通过不断积累实力，最终达到实力巅峰挑战体验最为完整boss战的时候，可是后期boss设计的种种问题让这种体验基本不存在。后期反倒是坐大牢的时候。但其它的boss设计上，由于需要玩家研究机制甚至是解谜类的boss比较多，基本上每个都有自己的特点，所以可以说boss的设计是比较精致的，也许会有相应的受众。 游戏整体的地图设计非常用心，氛围感提示感探索乐趣心流体验都不错。但是整个大地图上，传送系统解锁的非常晚，这导致游戏大部分时间需要浪费时间跑图。对地图的记忆力要求也非常高。地图结合战斗的话，整体的体验明显不如死细胞流畅，没有很爽快的推图感受。绝大多数地图的推图体验并不令人印象深刻。 解谜设计方面，比较反人类，给玩家的线索和引导学习的过程都不完善，属于拍脑袋定的解谜，“会的来，不会别玩”。 整个游戏的地图系统，这方面可能对于2d游戏来说是个硬性要求，也是个挑战。这个游戏的地图系统做的不是很完善，比如玩家自己打的标记似乎只能打一个，而且不能备注标记代表什么。2d游戏你前期肯定会看到很多后期才能到达的奖励，但是如果不能自定义标识，后期又没有故意设计好的行动路线让玩家再走一遍这个地图，就很难让玩家达成100%地图探索。对于强迫症来说，不太友善。但是玩2d类恶魔城游戏的，大多数又肯定有100%的诉求。 最后，由于这游戏太过小众国内基本没有攻略，所以分享几个游戏没有提示（比较坑人）的避坑点。 1.完美格挡和盾反找第一个boss依额涅多练练再推图。 2.老家的匕首战技npc，尽快买完他的技能然后第一时间杀掉。不然随着你游戏进程的推进他会把其他npc都杀掉，你低价买技能的机会就没了。最主要的是商人死了非常伤，会少天赋和强化材料。 3.中了扣除生命上限的诅咒，需要用万能灵药解。这个对应关系游戏内没有提示，网上也搜不到。关卡内npc会提示你找净化之泉，但我没见过这玩意。 4.有个天赋叫如果你是凡人，获得双倍精华。凡人应该指的是游戏内物品死神契约使用后可以解除这个契约，所谓变成凡人。但解除契约之后再死，直接删档。所以看不太懂这个天赋有啥意思，属于为了坑而坑。 5.跟老家雕像旁边的npc冥想，是重置属性点和天赋点的方式。冥想一开始不能有任何动作，直到场景变换。这个流程也没有任何提示。 6.意志力相关的说明，据说死多了意志力会增加，冥想可以降低意志力。意志力高于100影响结局选项。但游戏内没有提示。 7.好结局需要在进永生城之前杀够5个英雄boss，游戏内没有提示。我也是看其他攻略写的。 8.英雄boss如果无伤过会有5个黄金萝卜的奖励，不过大多数英雄boss就不是为了能无伤设计的，不必强求。

I would like to give the game a good review however the game has some glaring gameplay problems that prevents me from doing just that.   Number 1: Hitboxes are not choreographed correctly with their correlating animations. This results in the player being hit and taking damage because of no fault of their own as the player is being fed misinformation and can therefore not make the correct move in order to avoid being hit. The player should never need to learn that the animations are faulty and have to correct how they play because of it.  Number 2: Some of the bosses having attack combinations that the player simply can not dodge or block because of how said attacks interact with each other. An example of this is the boss "Wraith King Sirad" during his heroic boss battle. The boss can use one attack in which it throws fire at the ground that will spread outwards while disappearing from its starting point. Meanwhile three circles will appear at random points in the air that the player has to move inside of/tuch within a very limited time. If the player does not get to a circle before the time is up it will cause an explosion much bigger than the original circle that will cause a lot of damage to the player if hit. On its own this attack is very dodgeable, however, the boss also has an area of effect (AOE) attack that almost covers the whole arena that it can do straight after the aforementioned attack. The AOE attack can be blocked and it can be damaged (even easily with practice) but the player can NOT do either of these thing while in the air. Now take a wild guess how you avoid the fire and get to the circles? That's right, the player must jump, meaning that it is mechanically impossible for the player not to take damage from either one of the attacks if the boss does the AOE directly after. The player either gets hit by the fire for trying to doge/block the AOE and get to the circles, get hit by the circles for trying to doge/block the fire and the AOE, or gets hit by the AOE for trying to avoid the fire and get to the circles. When the player gets hit not because of their own fault but rather because of the design of the game, that is what is called artificial difficulty and is something a game such as Death's Gambit should viemently avoid doing as the whole point of the game is about player skill.  Number 3: The game does not inform the player that stat requerments only aply to the stats that the player has invested in levels and that the stats the player can get from equipments/items does not count towards this. This means that if the player finds a weapon that require 40 Fiennes that weapon can NOT be equipped if the player does not have 40 Fiennes that they have gained through leveling even though the player can have 40 Finnes where 20 of these stats come from equipment/items.

I tried hard to like this game but it's flawed. The premise is cool but the execution is not there. Some quick thoughts and nuisances:  1- The sequencing of where you have to go makes no sense. I go to a spot that is dark and the only lighting item in the game is gone because the NPC that sells it randomly died. Sunstones don't even bring any brightness. 2- The horse I thought was going to be more tactical but it's kinda useless. Also why do I have to open up a menu to call the horse when the only thing I can do at the horse station is call the horse. 3- Everything is way too expensive and pretty useless. You don't get enough essence from minions to save up for anything really. I kill a boss get 2000 essence, only for it to be slowly lost due to dying? Which isn't something they clarified would happen. 4- The stuff you do end up buying is not worth it. Saved up for a weapon, turns out next fight I get a weapon that does way more damage. And the crafting don't get me started "Immortalite Blade" does less damage then the stock sword sold by the other NPC but the crafting for it is relatively rare items. Why? And the passive on it isn't worth replacing the stock sword. 5- Don't even get me started on how glitched plumes are. I didn't die and I lost one.
